Largest Part of the Human Brain.Question: What is the largest part of the human brain?

Structure.Description: Two large, separate hemispheres on each side of the brain

Cerebral Cortex.Question: What is the surface of the cerebrum called?

Structure.Description: Deeply folded layer of nerve tissue
Structure.Purpose of Folds: Increase surface area, allowing more neurons and greater processing power

Frontal Lobes.Location: Front of the brain, above the eyes
Frontal Lobes.Function: Coordinate voluntary movement, speech, memory, emotion, higher cognition, and personality

Parietal Lobes.Location: Top of the brain, behind frontal lobes
Parietal Lobes.Function: Integrate sensory signals, process taste and some visual information

Occipital Lobes.Function: Process visual information, recognize colors and shapes

Temporal Lobes.Location: Sides of the brain, near the ears
Temporal Lobes.Function: Interpret auditory information and assist in visual processing

Limbic System.Question: What is the limbic system?
Limbic System.Answer: A group of structures that regulate emotion and motivation

Hippocampus.Location: Beneath the cerebral cortex in the temporal lobes

Thalamus.Function: Integrates sensory information and relays it to other brain regions

Hypothalamus.Function: Maintains homeostasis and sends hormonal signals; regulates hunger, thirst, and temperature

Midbrain.Function: Coordinates eye movements, inhibits unwanted movements, and integrates sensory and motor signals

Basal Ganglia.Definition: A neural network of structures in the midbrain and forebrain that regulate movement
Basal Ganglia.Function: Processes movement signals and sends feedback to the cortex

Parkinson’s Disease.Mechanism: Reduced dopamine signaling to basal ganglia

Cerebellum.Function: Coordinates movement, motor learning, spatial and temporal perception

Medulla.Function: Controls vital functions such as heart rate, breathing, and swallowing

Nerve Tracts.Definition: Bundles of nerve fibers connecting brain regions

Examples.Visual Pathway: Photoreceptors → Optic nerve → Optic tract → Thalamus → Visual cortex
Examples.Memory Networks: Hippocampus integrates memory with sensory input
Examples.Reflex Loops: Fast responses via spinal cord, no cortex involvement

Thalamocortical Loop.Definition: Two-way communication between thalamus and cortex

Pyramidal Cell.Structure: Cone-shaped body with branched dendrites and long axon

Circuits.Feedforward Inhibition: Excitation triggers inhibition of neighboring neurons
Circuits.Feedback Inhibition: Neurons activate inhibitory neurons that suppress earlier activity

Membrane Potential.Definition: Electrical potential across the neuron membrane

Action Potential.Definition: Electrical impulse traveling along the axon
Action Potential.Trigger: Occurs when threshold voltage is reached

Steroid Hormones.Mechanism: Act as transcription factors to alter gene expression
